programs.name: KanCare Frail Elderly Waiver (FE Waiver)
programs.what_it_does: The FE Waiver is a Medicaid home and community-based services (HCBS) waiver providing long-term supports to prevent nursing home placement. Services include adult day care, personal care, emergency response systems, medical equipment, and wellness monitoring delivered via managed care organizations.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Kansas residents aged 65+ at risk of institutionalization, meeting financial eligibility (via KanCare Clearinghouse) and functional needs (via ADRC); limited slots with waitlist.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Customized services based on needs; no fixed value, but covers cost of institutional care equivalent in home settings; limited enrollment.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: PACE provides comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community settings, including adult day health care and home care. It is administered through KDADS Aging Services to support frail elderly living independently.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Frail seniors aged 55+ (typically 65+) who are nursing home eligible and reside in PACE service areas.[1]
programs.benefit_value: All-inclusive capitated care covering Medicare and Medicaid services; value equivalent to institutional care.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) including QMB, SLMB, QI
programs.what_it_does: These programs help low-income Medicare beneficiaries pay premiums, deductibles, and co-pays via MIPPA outreach. Administered through KDADS with counseling support to enroll eligible seniors.[1][8]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors with Medicare meeting income/asset limits for Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), or Qualifying Individual (QI).[1][8]
programs.benefit_value: Covers Part B premiums ($174.70/month in 2024, adjusted annually), deductibles, and co-pays; QI up to $130/month per person.

programs.name: Food Assistance (SNAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly benefits via EBT card to purchase food for low-income households. Administered by Kansas Department of Children and Families (DCF).[3][7]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors, disabled, and families; seniors may qualify with higher income thresholds if unable to work.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per senior household; maximum varies by household size and income (e.g., up to $291/single in 2024, adjusted yearly).

programs.name: Low-Income Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers help with home heating and cooling bills, emergency aid, and weather-related crisis support. Funds distributed through local agencies via Kansas Housing Resources Corporation or DCF.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors meeting income guidelines (typically 150% FPL).[3]
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants $200-1,000 per season depending on fuel type, household size, and need.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides home energy efficiency improvements like insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs to reduce utility costs. Administered by Kansas Housing Resources Corporation (KHRC).[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income homeowners/renters including seniors in eligible rural/urban areas.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Free services valued at $3,000-7,000 per home; saves 10-20% on energy bills annually.

programs.name: Senior Health Insurance Counseling for Kansas (SHICK)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free, unbiased counseling on Medicare, supplements, and other health insurance options. Part of KDADS Aging Services including SMP for fraud prevention.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Kansas seniors and Medicare beneficiaries seeking insurance guidance.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; potential savings thousands in premiums/claims via proper enrollment.

programs.name: Kansas Senior Nutrition Program (Home-Delivered and Congregate Meals)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors and serves congregate meals at senior centers under Older Americans Act. Administered by KDADS through Area Agencies on Aging.[1][5]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 60+ who are homebound or attend congregate sites; some under 60 if with peers.[1]
programs.benefit_value: 5-14 meals/week; suggested donation $3-4/meal for those able; fully funded for neediest.

programs.name: Caregiver Support Programs
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite care, training, and support for family caregivers of seniors via Older Americans Act and Senior Care Act. Administered by KDADS Aging Services.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors 60+ or 55+ with Alzheimer's; income-based eligibility.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $1,600/year; counseling and training at no/low cost.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time paid community service work training leading to unsubsidized employment for low-income seniors. Funded under Older Americans Act via KDADS.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income seniors aged 55+ unable to find work.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Wages at minimum wage (~$7.25-15/hr) for 20 hrs/week; training and job placement.

programs.name: Kansas Legal Services (Legal Aid for Seniors)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal help for low-income seniors with benefits, housing, guardianship, and consumer issues. Refers to Kansas Guardianship Program for those needing court-appointed guardians.[5][7]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors (under 125% FPL typically) facing civil legal problems.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Free legal representation valued at $200+/hr; guardianship for vulnerable adults.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Operated through KDADS Area Agencies on Aging.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and board-and-care in Kansas.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy services; resolves 80%+ complaints without litigation.

programs.name: Senior Care Act Services
programs.what_it_does: Provides state-funded in-home and community-based services to help seniors remain independent and avoid institutionalization. Funds supportive services through contracts with area agencies on aging.[1][3]
programs.who_its_for: Kansas seniors aged 60+ who meet income and functional need eligibility criteria, prioritizing those at risk of nursing home placement.
programs.whats_unique: Kansas-specific state-funded program under the Senior Care Act, supplementing federal Older Americans Act programs with dedicated state resources not available nationwide.[1][3]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by service; includes personal care, homemaker, adult day care, and respite up to assessed need limits through state allocations.

programs.name: Senior Citizen Law Project (SCLP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides civil legal services to address income support, medical assistance, elder abuse, and other issues. Partners with KDADS and area agencies using OAA and state funds.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Kansas seniors aged 60+ facing civil legal needs, targeting those with greatest economic or social vulnerabilities.
programs.whats_unique: Kansas-specific project funded by state aging network, LSC, and OAA; focuses on elder rights protection not universally replicated.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Free legal representation and advice; secures benefits like MSPs worth thousands annually in Medicare cost savings.
